docs.title: Traum SOPs
docs.text: Welcome to the Traum Woodworking standard operating procedures wiki. Use the navigation on the left to browse by category.

docs.title: Sections
docs.text:
• General — Core standards and processes that span departments
  • Purchasing — Non-stock, stock, and receiving procedures
  • Engineering — Fabrication engineering standards and details
  • Fire Doors — 20- and 60-minute rated door manufacturing SOPs
  • Innergy — System setup, material cards, and project information
  • Production — Shop floor standards, samples, and tools

docs.title: File Naming
docs.text: Merged into File Standards.

docs.title: File Tree
docs.text: Merged into File Standards.

docs.title: SOP - Purchasing Process for Non-Stock Items
docs.text: See also: Purchasing Overview | Stock Purchasing | Receiving Process | Material Cards

docs.title: PURPOSE
docs.text: Ensure all non-stock materials are properly tracked, purchased, and received using a standardized process.

docs.title: RESPONSIBILITY
docs.text:
• Stockbiller: Create material cards and assign to BOM
  • Purchasing Agent: Generate and issue POs
  • Purchasing Manager: Receive materials correctly
  • Engineering/PM: Ensure materials are identified

docs.title: 1. Material Setup
docs.text:
1.1 Every purchased material must have a Material Card in Innergy

1.2 Material must be added to the Bill of Material (BOM) for the work order

1.3 If material does NOT exist in Innergy: - Stockbiller creates card with minimum required info - Purchasing completes/enhances card during quote/purchase

1.4 Drawer requests: - Must be placed in the Work Order Files tab for purchasing to retrieve

docs.title: 2. Materials to Buy (MTB)
docs.text:
2.1 The MTB Grid is the single source of truth for all non-stock purchasing

2.2 All materials must flow through: - Material Card → BOM → MTB Grid

docs.title: 3. Purchasing Process
docs.text:
3.1 Purchasing reviews MTB Grid

3.2 Generate Purchase Orders by: - Vendor grouping - Selecting “Create PO by Work Order”

docs.title: 4. Receiving
docs.text: 4.1 All non-stock items are: - Received directly to the Work Order

docs.title: 5. Overages (Stock Allocation)
docs.text:
5.1 If purchasing extra quantity (e.g., buying 40 when 4 are needed):

  • Required quantity → Work Order
  • Excess quantity → Inventory

5.2 Overages must be: - Allocated on the PO BEFORE submission - Received correctly into inventory

docs.title: Expectation
docs.text:
• MTB Grid is always accurate and up to date
  • No materials are purchased outside this process
  • Work orders receive correct quantities
  • Overages are properly allocated and tracked

docs.title: Purchasing Overview
docs.text: See also: Stock Purchasing | Non-Stock Purchasing | Receiving Process

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text:
• Detail the process for purchasing all materials within Traum's schedules
  • Ensure all materials can be purchased from one source of truth (Innergy)
  • Provide a framework for the Purchasing/Receiving department

docs.title: Non-Stock Materials
docs.text:
See Non-Stock Purchasing for the full process. Non-stock materials flow through:

Material Card → BOM → MTB Grid → PO → Received to Work Order

docs.title: Stock Materials
docs.text:
See Stock Purchasing for the full process. Stock materials flow through:

MTB Grid (Stock View) → PO → Received to Inventory → Request & Fulfill

docs.title: Receiving
docs.text: See Receiving Process for the full process.

docs.title: Exceptions
docs.text:
The following do not flow through Innergy:

  • Office supplies
  • Verbal orders (shop floor/office)

In these cases, a written ticket must be filled out and given to the purchasing manager to document who, what, and when.

docs.title: SOP - Purchasing & Request Process for Stock Items
docs.text: See also: Purchasing Overview | Non-Stock Purchasing | Receiving Process

docs.title: PURPOSE
docs.text: Define the process for purchasing stock materials and requesting them for use on the shop floor.

docs.title: RESPONSIBILITY
docs.text:
• Purchasing Agent: Purchase stock materials for inventory
  • Purchasing Manager: Receive and distribute inventory
  • Shop Floor: Request and pull materials from inventory
  • Stockbiller/Engineering: Ensure materials are properly flagged as stock

docs.title: 1. Stock Material Setup
docs.text: 1.1 Materials designated as stock must be: - Marked as Stock Items on the Material Card in Innergy

docs.title: 2. Purchasing Stock Items
docs.text:
2.1 Purchasing reviews the MTB Grid (Stock View)

2.2 Purchase materials for: - Inventory replenishment - General shop use (not tied to a specific work order)

docs.title: 3. Receiving Stock Items
docs.text: 3.1 All stock materials are: - Received into Inventory (not to a work order)

docs.title: 4. Requesting Stock Items (Shop Floor)
docs.text:
4.1 To pull materials from inventory, the shop must:

  • Use Request & Fulfill, OR
  • Submit a written ticket including:
  • Material description
  • Quantity
  • Work order (if applicable)

4.2 Ticket must be given to: - Purchasing Manager / Purchasing Department

docs.title: 5. Fulfillment
docs.text: 5.1 Purchasing fulfills request by: - Pulling material from inventory - Allocating to the correct work order (if required)

docs.title: Expectation
docs.text:
• All stock items are purchased through the MTB Grid
  • Inventory is accurately maintained
  • No materials are taken without request/fulfillment
  • Shop communicates all needs clearly through the defined process

docs.title: Receiving Process
docs.text:
See also: Purchasing Overview | Stock Purchasing | Non-Stock Purchasing

🚧 Full SOP pending documentation.

docs.title: Stock Items
docs.text: All stock materials are received into Inventory (not to a work order). See Stock Purchasing for the full flow.

docs.title: Non-Stock Items
docs.text: All non-stock items are received directly to the Work Order. See Non-Stock Purchasing for the full flow, including overage allocation rules.

docs.title: Rollout Shelf Sizing
docs.text: Merged into Rollout Standards.

docs.title: Drafting Kanban — Labor Card
docs.text: This SOP determines who/when a drafting labor kanban card is created, completed, etc.

docs.title: Creation
docs.text: • The labor kanban card for drafting is created via the Work Orders process. Reminder: at least one hour of drafting labor must be attached to a work order in order for it to show up in the Labor Kanban.

docs.title: Queue
docs.text:
• The card is moved to the assigned drafter.
  • Queue is reviewed in the weekly engineering meeting.
  • The drafter works on shop drawings in descending order in their queue.
  • Note: submittals process no longer captured in engineering meeting. See Submittals.

docs.title: Closing
docs.text:
• When an RFC drawing is published, the drafter must move the card for the room to "drafting complete" labor step in the kanban.
  • The project manager is responsible for the Release to Stockbilling process, which follows the drafting complete step.

docs.title: Shop Drawings
docs.text:
Format: {Area}_ Room Number_ Room-Name_ Rev#_ Submittal Type_ Date

Examples:

MH 116B Guest Bath Rev0 For Submittal 11-26-24

011 Dressing Room Rev2 For Approval 12-2-23

Notes: - The Area prefix is not on all drawings — skip it unless explicitly determined by the PM. - There should only be ONE shop drawing in the \3. Engineering-Production\ folder at any time. Old PDF copies go into the \Archive of the room folder. - The Production folder should contain NO shop drawings until marked by the Stockbiller at time of release. - Acceptable Submittal Types, in order:

For Submittal → For Approval → RFC

docs.title: Room Folders
docs.text:
Format: Room-Number_ Room/Scope Name

Examples:

Kitchen Island

002b Guest Bath

Lower Level

Notes: - Do not include the Job Number in room folder names — it clutters the view. Room folders use Room Name and Scope Items only.

docs.title: Project-Level: 3. Engineering-Production
docs.text:
Each room or drafted scope item gets its own folder.

To create a new room folder: 1. Navigate to \3.Engineering-Production\! General 2. Find the room folder template 3. Copy and paste it into the parent folder 4. Rename following the naming convention above

docs.title: Finish Callouts- Shop Drawings
docs.text: This SOP is to determine the standard for finish callouts and schedules for shop drawings in all cases

docs.title: Finish Schedule
docs.text:
• Each project will have a finish schedule of ALL finishes currently approved/sampling for the project
  • Wherever possible, the schedule made by Traum team should match the finishes in the architectural drawings, for clarity; however, priority needs to be made that all drawings released to the shop have a unified schedule internally (i.e., two drawings for the same job cannot both say WD-1 if the finish is different)
  • Each drawing will have a finish schedule of the finishes captured in its scope only

docs.title: Finish Callouts
docs.text:
• Pt. Grade material is to be called out as PT
  • If painted by Traum, finish schedule must say "Paint by Traum"
  • In finish schedule, PT material to be "mill option", for clarity

docs.title: Notes
docs.text: • Please see Sample Process for finish callouts on shop releases, as well as the process for sampling

docs.title: SOP - Internal Review of Shop Drawings
docs.text: See also: Submittals | Drafting Kanban | File Standards

docs.title: PURPOSE
docs.text: Ensure all shop drawings are reviewed internally for accuracy and completeness prior to client submittal.

docs.title: RESPONSIBILITY
docs.text:
• Draftsperson: Initiate and revise drawings
  • Project Manager (PM): Review and provide redlines
  • Head of Engineering: Review and provide redlines

docs.title: 1. Initial Review Notification
docs.text:
1.1 Draftsperson notifies: - Project Manager - Head of Engineering

1.2 Provide drawing set for review

docs.title: 2. Redline Collection
docs.text:
2.1 PM and Head of Engineering: - Review drawings - Provide redlines/comments

2.2 Draftsperson collects and consolidates all feedback

docs.title: 3. Revision
docs.text: 3.1 Draftsperson updates drawings based on redlines

docs.title: 4. Final Internal Check
docs.text:
4.1 Revised drawing set is sent back to: - Project Manager - Head of Engineering

4.2 Both parties confirm drawings are ready for submittal

docs.title: 5. Submittal Process
docs.text: 5.1 Once approved internally: - PM proceeds with Submittal process

docs.title: Expectation
docs.text:
• No drawings are submitted without both reviews
  • All redlines are addressed prior to submittal
  • Final set is verified before release
  • PM tracks submittal status per standard process

docs.title: Checklist
docs.text: • The following checklist is shown in Innergy, when the drafting work order moves from "RFC" to "Cutlisting" step- it is a requirement that all items are checked in Innergy

docs.title: Flow
docs.text: • If the process is followed correctly, the associated work order in production will automatically move to the "Long Lead Purchasing" step; ensure that the card moves to Release to Stockbilling and has one hour minimum of Stockbilling labor item attached. Otherwise, this card will note appear in the Stockbilling queue

docs.title: Rollout Standoff Standards
docs.text: Merged into Rollout Standards.

docs.title: Submittals
docs.text: See also: Internal Review | Drafting Kanban | Release to Stockbilling

docs.title: Submittal Log
docs.text:
• All Shop drawing submittals must have an associated Submittal in the Innergy project
  • Submittal status allows Project Manager to track the submittal out to client
  • When submitted, change status to submitted
  • When returned, mark status of the return
  • If the submittal returns as Revise and Resubmit, use the menu on the submittal grid (three dots) to 'Resubmit'
  • Otherwise (AAN or Approved status) the submittal can be edited to show RFC status on release for construction

docs.title: Creating a submittal
docs.text:
• Go to the submittals tab
  • Select Create above the grid
  • Name the submittal for the Drafting Work Order/ scope area, following the file standards convention
  • Add the shop drawings/pdf (not required, helpful for tracking revisions)

docs.title: SOP: Material Card Naming Standards in Innergy
PURPOSE
docs.text: To standardize material card naming conventions in Innergy, ensuring consistency, clarity, and improved usability for engineering, estimating, and production.

docs.title: RESPONSIBILITY
docs.text:
Engineering and Engineering Support teams are responsible for: - Creating and maintaining material cards in accordance with this standard - Verifying naming conventions during setup and review

Estimating and Production teams are responsible for: - Using standardized material cards - Reporting inconsistencies or deviations

docs.title: Procedure
docs.text:
1. General Naming Convention Rule 1.1 All material names must follow:
  2. Largest → Smallest category sequencing 1.2 Names should be:
  3. Clear and descriptive
  4. Consistent across all material types

  5. Free of unnecessary abbreviations unless standardized

  6. Lumber Material Naming 2.1 Lumber material cards must follow this order:

  7. Specie → Cut → Size → Grade (if applicable)

2.2 Example format: - White Oak, Rift, 4/4, B Grade - Maple, P/S, 8/4 please note that A grade is the standard; the only grade on material card should be below standard quality

2.3 Guidelines: - Specie must always be listed first - Cut (Rift, Quartersawn, Plainsawn) must follow - Size must be in standard lumber thickness notation - Grade included only when relevant

  1. Sheet Material Naming 3.1 Sheet goods must follow this order:
  2. Material → Special Quality → Decimal Size (Fraction size in parentheses)

3.2 Example format: - MDF, Marine, 0.75" (3/4") - Plywood, ACX, 0.5" (1/2")

3.3 Guidelines: - Use decimal thickness as the primary value - Include fractional equivalent in parentheses - Special qualities (MR, FR, VC, etc.) must be clearly defined and standardized

  1. Formatting Standards 4.1 Use standard capitalization (no all caps) 4.2 Separate categories with commas 4.3 Maintain consistent spacing after commas 4.4 Do not vary order under any circumstances

  2. Verification 5.1 During creation or review of material cards, confirm:

  3. Correct category order is followed
  4. Naming matches existing standards
  5. No duplicate or conflicting material names exist

docs.title: Expectation
docs.text:
• All material cards in Innergy follow a consistent, predictable structure
  • Users can quickly identify materials without confusion or interpretation
  • Errors in estimating, engineering, and production caused by unclear naming are minimized

docs.title: PURPOSE:
docs.text: To establish Innergy as the single source of truth for all project material and finish information. To standardize what must be entered into the Budget tab prior to PM handoff in order to reduce downstream errors in purchasing, cutbilling, production, installation, and stockbilling.

docs.title: RESPONSIBILITY:
docs.text:
Estimator / Project Manager (Shared Responsibility)

The Estimator is responsible for accurate initial entry at project setup. The Project Manager is responsible for verification and completeness at time of handoff and prior to release to production.

docs.title: Procedure:
docs.text:
1. Enter the Finish Schedule into the Innergy Budget tab at time of project setup.
    1. Unique finishes get their own code, regardless of ID/Arch. finish callouts
    2. Finishes must be clearly identifiable and usable for work order assignment.
  2. Input all standard, and priced, hardware into the Budget tab. These items may be marked as "approved" at project start
    1. Include all standard hinges.
    2. Include all drawer glides.
    3. Include any specified hardware types unique to the project.
  3. Input all non-standard or custom hardware/material items. These will be marked approved at the time of an approved drawing (or, if requiring external approval, on signed approval)
    1. Custom brass hang rods.
    2. Specialty metal components.
    3. Any hardware not considered a standard company hinge/slide.
  4. Verify that work orders are properly assigned to their corresponding finishes.
    1. Each work order must clearly reflect the finish it is to receive.
  5. PM review each work order prior to handoff with stockbilling.
    1. Confirm completeness of hardware.
    2. Confirm completeness of finish schedule.
    3. Confirm alignment with estimate and approved scope.

docs.title: Expectation
docs.text:
Innergy shall serve as the definitive and reliable source of project material and finish information.

Any cutbiller or project-level team member must be able to: - Identify all hinges and drawer glides used across the project. - Identify all custom or non-standard hardware items. - Identify all finishes used on the project. - Identify which work orders are assigned to each finish.

There shall be: - No reliance on tribal knowledge. - No internal clarification RFIs regarding hardware or finish selections. - No missing hardware discovered at cutbill stage due to incomplete Budget tab entry. - No ambiguity regarding project finish assignments.

docs.title: Drafting
docs.text:
1. Go to the project page in Innergy. Select "Budget" tab.
  2. The Budget> Products page will automatically be open.
  3. Select the products that pertain to the work order you are going to make (i.e., an entire room of scope items, just the base cabinets, all the doors, etc.)
  4. Select "Create Work Order" just above the grid.
  5. Name the work order, following the naming convention showed below
  6. Select the "Drafting" Type, and set the critical submittal date (automatic dates are generated, but may not be accurate to the needs of the project)
  7. Click the green button to create the work order

docs.title: Solids
docs.text: • When "panelizing" solid lumber material, using the same species as backer for balancing is required.

docs.title: Veneer
docs.text:
• All veneer must be balanced with the same thickness material
    • This means that if a specialty thickness is ordered for a job, purchasing must be notified to buy backer in the same specialty thickness
  • All pressed panels must have an odd number of parts
  • Grain direction must be the same on both sides
  • Backer should have properties that complement face veneer; comparable shrinkage & expansion rates (specific gravity)

docs.title: Basic Considerations
docs.text:
• 3/4" Plywood, doubled top & bottom plates
  • Loose shipping cleat on bottom, 1 3/4" thick (1/16" narrower than die wall width)
  • No more than 96" in a run
  • 1/2" sheeting either side
  • Solid wood studs where brackets are required
  • Weight- to be assessed by stockbillers; assess span, countertops, overhang, etc.

docs.title: Sizes; unless otherwise specified
docs.text:
• 2x4 size: standard size
  • 2x6 size: standard for drains/wet wall

docs.title: Finished Bottom Panel
docs.text: In the case where a group of cabinets are "ganged" together, and the bottom panel runs through the entire "gang", the following detail is the standard.

docs.title: Handoff
docs.text: This SOP details and structures the handoff meeting between Engineering and Production

docs.title: When
docs.text: Weekly meeting scheduled; cancelled if there are no new releases to the shop to walk through

docs.title: Who
docs.text:
• Shop leadership; mill, sheets, assembly, finish
  • veneering if decided necessary
  • Engineering
  • Project Manager of release(s)
  • Stockbiller of release(s)
  • Drafter

docs.title: Agenda
docs.text:
• Releases ordered due to critical date/ release order

  • Critical dates; any hard dates, any special deadlines, expedite, etc.

  • Scope overview;
  • Overall details:
    • Stile and rail doors or flat panel
    • Solids vs veneer
  • Purchasing
    • Long lead items/order status
    • Needs for lumber/veneer; order status
  • Finish samples brought to meeting so the shop understands finish before release
  • Workflow type; cabinetry, paneling, doors, etc.
  • Special considerations for scope; glass, leather, fabric, metal, etc.
  • Details
  • Profiles, drawer fronts, interactions
  • Shop breakdown
  • Should this scope area/room be released to the shop in separate releases?
  • What is the breakdown?
  • Special considerations

docs.title: Hardware Notes
docs.text: This page has been merged into Hardware Standards.

docs.title: Hardware Standards
docs.text:
This SOP determines the hardware to be used unless otherwise specified by a project team.

See also: Hinge Placement for centerline dimensions and hinge count guidelines.

docs.title: Cabinet Hinges
docs.text: All cabinet hinges to be Salice brand; existing projects that used the Blum spec will continue.

docs.title: Full Overlay
docs.text:
• C2PBAE9
  • w/ 2mm plate BAR3R29

docs.title: Half Overlay
docs.text:
• C2PBGE9
  • w/ 2mm plate BAR3R29

docs.title: Inset
docs.text:
• C2PBPE9
  • w/ 3mm plate BAR3R39

docs.title: Drawer Slides
docs.text: • All drawer slides are also to be Salice brand where applicable

docs.title: Shelf Pins
docs.text:
• 5mm shelf pins
  • Adj shelves drawn with three holes, 32mm spacing between each — one above shelf location, one below

docs.title: Shop Drawing Hardware Notes
docs.text: • Hardware in the shop drawing must be verified by the engineer before reaching Stockbilling.

docs.title: Hardware Ordered by Traum
docs.text: • All hardware to be ordered by Traum must appear in the Hardware tab in the CAD drawing.

docs.title: Hardware Excluded
docs.text: • All hardware that is present but not to be ordered by Traum must appear in a tab Hardware by Others in the CAD drawing.

docs.title: SOP – Hinge Placement
docs.text:
Note on hinge brand: The current standard is Salice hinges. Blum spec applies to existing projects already under Blum. See Hardware Standards for part numbers.

All measurements to the centerline of hinge.

  • 3.5" from bottom of cabinet door
  • 3.5" from top of cabinet door
  • Intermediate hinges spaced evenly

docs.title: Hinge Count Guidelines
docs.text:
• 2 hinges: up to 36" height or ≤ 15 lb
  • 3 hinges: up to 62" height or ≤ 30 lb
  • 4 hinges: up to 84" height or ≤ 45 lb
  • 5 hinges: up to 96" height or ≤ 60 lb

docs.title: Exceptions
docs.text:
In any exception, all hinge placements must be dimensioned in drawings for shop clarity.

  • If a hinge interferes with a rollout, shelf, or interior component, move the offending hinge.
  • If there is a rollout at the bottom of the cabinet, move the bottom hinge to 4.5" from the bottom of the door; others should be evenly spaced.
  • If a sink cabinet has an apron on the interior, move the top hinge to 1" below the bottom of the apron panel. Add a second hinge 2.5" below the top hinge (3.25" for Salice), then evenly space remaining hinges. See also: Sink Aprons.
  • If door width exceeds door height, add a second hinge at the top, placed 2.5" below the top hinge, and evenly space remaining hinges.

Apron Panel Spec: Maximum desired interior apron height: 6"

See also: Rollout Standards

docs.title: PURPOSE
docs.text: To eliminate unnecessary field delays, return trips to supply houses, and remake orders by proactively including a standard supplemental kit with each job's first shipment. This kit provides installers with the blocking material, shim stock, and finish touch-up supplies needed to handle minor field adjustments without sourcing materials on-site or escalating to the shop.

docs.title: RESPONSIBILITY
docs.text:
• Process Owner: Engineering / Production Management
  • Finish Kit Coordination: Steven (approves finish contents and match per job)
  • Kit Assembly & Listing: Stock fillers / shipping team (responsible for including kit line items on cut lists and BOMs)
  • Project Manager: Responsible for communicating job-specific kit needs and managing replenishment requests mid-job
  • Installer: Responsible for notifying the Project Manager when kit materials are running low

docs.title: PROCEDURE
docs.text:
1. For every new job, include the following standard installer kit items with the first shipment:
  2. Blocking Plywood
    1. Include one (1) 2-foot rip of ¾" plywood, approximately 8 inches wide.
    2. This material is for field blocking, shimming, and minor build-outs as needed by the installer.
    3. If the Project Manager determines a full sheet is more appropriate for a large job, a full sheet may be substituted and noted on the shipment.
  3. Shim Stock
    1. Pre-cut shim stock in standard thicknesses (1/16", 1/8", and other standard increments as applicable).
    2. Diego is responsible for cutting shim stock to spec.
    3. Shim stock must be listed as a line item on the cut list for the first shipment.
  4. Finish Touch-Up Kit
    1. One kit is required per unique approved finish on the job.
    2. Touch-up kits ship with the first release of each finish — not with every subsequent shipment.
    3. Kit contents are determined in coordination with Steven and may include any combination of the following:
      • Small bottle of finish with catalyst packaged separately (to be mixed on-site)
      • Wax filler stick matched to the finish color
      • Touch-up marker or stain marker (pre-kit based, as applicable)
    4. Steven must review and approve kit contents and finish match before inclusion on any shipment.
    5. Touch-up kits must be listed as a shippable line item on the first applicable work order.
  5. Hardware finishes must be accurately reflected on all BOMs with finish-specific notation.
  6. Non-standard finishes (e.g., black vs. nickel) must include a finish callout note alongside the part number — not just the base part number.
  7. Stock fillers are responsible for identifying finish-specific part numbers or flagging discrepancies before releasing a BOM.
  8. If a finish-specific part number is unknown, the stock filler must flag the item and resolve it before the BOM is released, not after the order is placed.
  9. Mid-job replenishment:
  10. If an installer runs low on blocking material, shim stock, or touch-up supplies during a job, they must notify the Project Manager.
  11. The Project Manager adds the replenishment items to the next scheduled shipment.
  12. Installers must not make independent trips to local suppliers for materials that should have been shipped from the shop without first notifying their Project Manager.
  13. Installer-to-PM communication:
  14. Installers must keep the Project Manager informed of any field material needs, shortages, or modifications required on-site.
  15. Field modifications that require remade parts must be communicated to the PM before the installer proceeds, where possible.

docs.title: EXPECTATION
docs.text: Every first shipment for a new job includes the standard installer kit (blocking plywood, shim stock, and finish touch-up materials). Remakes due to missing blocking, field supply runs, and finish mismatches caused by BOM errors are reduced to near zero. Hardware BOMs accurately reflect specified finishes with correct part numbers and finish notations. Installers have what they need to handle minor field issues independently, and Project Managers are kept informed of any material needs throughout the job.

docs.title: PURPOSE
docs.text: To ensure that cabinet door and component reveals maintain a premium appearance when cabinet exteriors are painted and interiors use a contrasting material or color. This SOP establishes the process for selecting edgebanding that minimizes visual contrast at door reveals and other exposed edges.

docs.title: RESPONSIBILITY
docs.text:
Project Manager (PM) Responsible for reviewing cabinet interior and exterior finishes and determining the appropriate edgebanding color when the exterior finish is painted.

Engineering / Drafting Responsible for documenting the selected edgebanding clearly in shop drawings and cutlists.

Production (Parts / Assembly) Responsible for applying the specified edgebanding per drawings and confirming materials match the documented specification.

docs.title: Procedure
docs.text:
1. Identify Painted Exterior Conditions

  2. During project review and engineering kickoff, identify all cabinetry where the exterior finish is painted.

  3. Confirm the interior material and color (e.g., walnut veneer, maple melamine, prefinished plywood, etc.).

  4. Evaluate Potential Reveal Contrast

  5. Review typical reveal conditions including:

  6. Door-to-case reveals

  7. Drawer front reveals
  8. End panel edges
  9. Applied panels and fillers

  10. Any visible cabinet component edges

  11. Determine whether the standard interior-matching edgeband will be visually prominent when viewed from the exterior.

  12. Determine Edgebanding Strategy

When exterior paint and interior materials create a noticeable contrast, the PM must determine the appropriate edgebanding color using one of the following approaches:

Option A – Exterior-Matching Edgebanding (Preferred when reveals are highly visible)

  • Edgebanding matches or closely approximates the exterior paint color.
  • Used to visually minimize the reveal line.

Option B – Interior-Matching Edgebanding (Standard Condition)

  • Edgebanding matches the interior material or color.
  • Acceptable when the reveal is minimal or not visually prominent.

Option C – Neutral / Transitional Edgebanding (When neither match is ideal)

  • Select a neutral tone that softens the contrast between interior and exterior materials.

  • Project Manager Decision

  • The PM must review the cabinet configuration and make a deliberate edgebanding selection decision whenever painted exteriors and contrasting interiors occur.

  • Example condition:

  • Exterior: Painted White

  • Interior: Walnut

  • Decision: Use white edgebanding on cabinet case edges at door reveals to prevent dark lines appearing around doors.

  • Document the Selection

  • Engineering must clearly document the chosen edgebanding in:

  • Shop drawings

  • Cutlists

  • Material specifications

  • Notes should specify:

  • Edgebanding color

  • Areas where it applies (e.g., case front edges, applied panels, fillers).

  • Production Verification

  • Prior to machining and edgebanding, production should verify:

  • The specified edgebanding matches the drawings.

  • The correct edgeband is staged for the project.

docs.title: Expectation
docs.text: Traum cabinetry is expected to maintain a premium visual standard. Visible reveals that create harsh contrast between cabinet interiors and painted exteriors are unacceptable unless specifically approved. Project Managers must proactively select edgebanding that minimizes these contrasts and ensures a refined, cohesive appearance.

docs.title: Rollout Standards
docs.text: See also: Hinge Placement | Hardware Standards

docs.title: PURPOSE
docs.text: Ensure proper clearance for rollout shelves/drawers inside closed cabinets by accounting for rear blocking.

docs.title: RESPONSIBILITY
docs.text:
• Engineering/Drafting: Apply correct sizing in drawings
  • Shop: Build per drawings
  • Purchasing: Purchase rollouts per drawings

docs.title: Procedure
docs.text:
1. Identify rollout shelves/drawers inside closed cabinets.
  2. Increase depth of rollout by 5/8" over standard sizing.
  3. This adjustment accounts for rear blocking and proper slide function/clearance.
  4. Apply consistently unless otherwise noted.

docs.title: Expectation
docs.text:
• All applicable rollouts are sized correctly in drawings.
  • No field modification required due to blocking interference.

docs.title: PURPOSE
docs.text: Standardize the sizing of standoffs on rollout drawers across all engineering drawings, ensuring consistency, proper hinge clearance, and improved manufacturing efficiency.

docs.title: RESPONSIBILITY
docs.text: • Engineering: Apply correct standoff dimensions based on hinge type in all shop drawings; verify during drawing review; no deviation without formal approval.

docs.title: Procedure
docs.text:
1. Determine Hinge Type
  2. Inset
  3. Full Overlay

  4. Half Overlay

  5. Standard Standoff Height

  6. All rollout drawer standoffs: 2" tall (no exceptions)

  7. Standard Standoff Width by Hinge Type

  8. Inset → 1 1/2" wide
  9. Full overlay → 1" wide

  10. Half overlay → 1 1/4" wide

  11. Modeling & Drawing

  12. Show standoffs in plan, elevation, and/or section views as needed.
  13. Dimension standoffs explicitly — do not rely on assumptions.

  14. All blocking should run the full length of the cabinet interior.

  15. Verification

  16. Confirm correct hinge type identified, correct width applied, height consistently 2".

docs.title: Expectation
docs.text:
• Shop and assembly teams can rely on uniform dimensions without rework or clarification.
  • Hinge clearance and drawer interference errors are eliminated.

docs.title: Sample Sizing
docs.text: This page has been merged into Samples Process.

docs.title: Sample Request
docs.text: TBD

docs.title: Sample Sizing (FedEx Shipping)
docs.text:
• All samples should be produced at a maximum of 11" wide, sent to the finish room long, then cut down to 11" lengths for submission; maximum 17" lengths.
  • Unless otherwise specified, all samples should fit into a Large FedEx box with packaging.
  • Inside dimensions of the box: 12 3/8" x 3" x 17 1/2"

docs.title: Sampling Length/Process
docs.text:
A standard wood sample blank should be 11" x 96"; a minimum of 7 samples will be cut from each blank:

  1. After any raw treatments (resaw, wirebrushing, etc.), before sent to finish room — [Mill Copy]
  2. Step sample detailing finish for the finish room
  3. Finished sample for Assembly storyboard
  4. Finished sample for Office storyboard / sample room
  5. (3) Finished samples for submittal to client

Each sample receives a label with the project code, date, finish version (e.g. WD-4.2), and space for signature upon approval.

  • Upon client approval, PM signs the other copies and distributes to shop locations above.
  • Once a job is complete, samples can be collected for the archive.

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• Any solid wood sample must receive the same treatment (7 samples, one raw for the mill, labeled, distributed).
  • Other material samples (metal, glass, solid surface, etc.) should have the minimum copies necessary for the work done in the shop — e.g., if assembling and finishing a metal material, assembly and finish each need a copy, as well as the office.

docs.title: Finish Callouts in Innergy
docs.text:
• All shop releases should have a finish callout that matches what is input in Innergy.
  • Innergy's finish codes should match the client's finish codes/schedule whenever possible.
  • Any change to a finish in shop must come with an amended finish callout to the shop set of drawings in all cases.
  • Every work order should have finishes assigned to it in Innergy, wherever possible.

docs.title: Sample Labeling
docs.text: TBD

docs.title: Sample SKU
docs.text: TBD

docs.title: Shelf Setback Standards
docs.text: 🚧 This SOP is pending documentation.

docs.title: Engineering Standard – Sheppard (In-Wall) Brackets with Side-to-Side Adjustability
PURPOSE
docs.text: To standardize the engineering of Sheppard (in-wall) brackets to include side-to-side adjustability, allowing for minor field scribing without requiring bracket remounting.

docs.title: RESPONSIBILITY
docs.text:
• Engineering Team: Incorporate adjustability into all applicable bracket designs and shop drawings.
  • Project Managers (PMs): Ensure project scope and installation conditions are communicated to engineering.
  • Shop and Installation Teams: Use the adjustability as intended during installation and report any limitations or issues.

docs.title: Procedure
docs.text:
1. Bracket Design Requirements
  2. All Sheppard (in-wall) brackets shall include side-to-side adjustability.

  3. Design must allow for a total tolerance of ±1/8" horizontal adjustment.

  4. Engineering Implementation

  5. Incorporate slotted holes, oversized holes, or equivalent hardware solutions to achieve the required adjustment range.
  6. Ensure adjustability does not compromise structural integrity or load requirements.

  7. Clearly indicate adjustable features in shop drawings and details.

  8. Shop Drawing Documentation

  9. Call out:
    • Adjustment range (±1/8")
    • Fastener type and size
    • Slot or hole dimensions

  10. Include installation notes stating that brackets are designed for minor scribing adjustments without remounting.

  11. Coordination with Installation

  12. Communicate the intent of adjustability to installers.

  13. Ensure installation sequence allows adjustment before final fastening.

  14. Quality Check

  15. Engineering review must confirm adjustability is included before release.
  16. QC must verify fabricated brackets match engineered adjustability details.

docs.title: Expectation
docs.text: All engineered Sheppard (in-wall) brackets shall provide ±1/8" side-to-side adjustment so installers can make minor scribing adjustments without removing or remounting the bracket, reducing field rework and improving installation efficiency.

docs.title: Entryway
docs.text: • All Shippable Items should be considered for entry into the residence, as well as the pathway for the specific room. Considerations to be made by the engineer with feedback from the field.

docs.title: Truck size
docs.text: • The opening to the large shipping truck is 92" x 98"- anything larger than this opening should be split into two items

docs.title: Item size
docs.text:
• Any shippable item that is larger than 120" x 48", regardless of the combination of the dimensions, should be considered for size reduction for shipping and unloading.
  • Full height cabinets w/o a toekick must be able to stand up in room, corner to corner, for install

docs.title: Item Weight
docs.text: • Any shippable item that is calculated to weigh over 300 lb. should be considered for size reduction for shipping and unloading.

docs.title: Traum Tools Policy
docs.text: • This SOP defines company policy on tools; which are provided by Traum, which are provided by the employee, and the policies regarding each

docs.title: Traum-Provided Tools
docs.text:
• Traum will provide all corded tools required for a position's duties.
  • Traum will provide the following cordless tools in a kit for the employee/position;
    1. Circular Saw
    2. Router
    3. Sander
    4. These tools are to be returned in like/similar condition upon termination of employment. Any misuse/unusual wear on Traum provided tools will be compensated by employee at return.
    5. These tools are provided dependent on position

docs.title: Employee-Provided Tools
docs.text: • Employees are expected to provide their own cordless drills/drivers

docs.title: General Notes
docs.text:
• Any bits/blades/pads required in the scope of work will be provided by Traum
  • Traum provided tools are Makita-branded; should an employee desire a tool to fit a different brand's battery, that tool must be provided by the employee

docs.title: PURPOSE
docs.text: To standardize the construction and detailing of sink aprons to ensure consistent appearance, eliminate visible seams, and improve overall product quality.

docs.title: RESPONSIBILITY
docs.text:
Engineering team is responsible for: - Applying this standard to all sink apron designs in shop drawings. - Clearly detailing apron and top relationships. - Verifying compliance during drawing review.

Production team is responsible for: - Building sink apron assemblies per drawings. - Ensuring joinery follows the intended design.

docs.title: Procedure
docs.text:
1. Sink Apron Orientation 1.1 All sink aprons must:
  2. Run horizontally across the cabinet front

  3. Be continuous across the width of the sink cabinet unless otherwise specified

  4. Top and Apron Relationship 2.1 The cabinet top must die into the sink apron 2.2 The sink apron must remain:

  5. Continuous and uninterrupted at the front face

  6. Prohibited Condition 3.1 Do NOT design or build:

  7. The sink apron dying into the cabinet top 3.2 This condition is not allowed because:
  8. It creates a visible seam at the top of the cabinet

  9. It reduces visual quality on a high-visibility component

  10. Modeling & Drawing Requirements 4.1 Clearly show:

  11. The sink apron as a continuous horizontal member
  12. The cabinet top terminating into the apron 4.2 Include sections where necessary to clarify:

  13. Joinery condition between top and apron 4.3 Avoid ambiguous detailing—this condition must be obvious in drawings

  14. Verification 5.1 During internal review, confirm:

  15. Apron runs full horizontal width
  16. Cabinet top dies into apron (not vice versa)
  17. No exposed seam exists at the top front edge

docs.title: Expectation
docs.text:
• All sink apron cabinets have a clean, seamless front appearance
  • No visible seams occur at the top of the cabinet
  • Engineering and production maintain consistent, repeatable detailing across all projects

docs.title: SOP - Solid Wood Edge Application (Tongue vs Applied)
PURPOSE
docs.text: Define a clear standard for when solid wood edges are tongued vs. applied to ensure quality, alignment, and install efficiency.

docs.title: RESPONSIBILITY
docs.text:
• Engineering/Drafting: Define edge condition in drawings
  • Shop: Execute per drawings and SOP
  • Project Management: Ensure consistency and resolve exceptions

docs.title: 1. Standard Rule
docs.text:
1.1 Solid wood edges applied to panels (MDF/plywood) are tongued by default.

1.2 This includes:

  • Drop edges
  • Face frames
  • Solid edges on panel products

1.3 Reason:

  • Stronger joint
  • Better alignment
  • Easier installation
  • Matches premium quality standards

docs.title: 2.1 Typical Condition
docs.text:
• Use one fixed tongue + one sliding side when spanning cabinets
  • Provides alignment while allowing easier install

docs.title: 2.2 Double Tongue
docs.text:
• Allowed when:

  • Members are wide enough (>1.5")

  • Tolerance is built in
  • Avoid if fit-up becomes difficult

docs.title: 3. Prefinished Interiors
docs.text:
3.1 Use one side tongued, one side loose

3.2 Reason:

  • Prevents damage to prefinished surfaces
  • Avoids sanding/refinishing issues

docs.title: 4. When Applied (No Tongue) is Acceptable
docs.text:
Use applied edges when:

  • Metal or decorative trim is involved
  • Material layers interfere (e.g., cork)
  • Finish sensitivity makes tongue risky
  • Installation would be overly difficult with a tongue

docs.title: 5. Large or Tall Assemblies
docs.text:
5.1 Use:

  • Single tongue + sliding cabinet (preferred), OR
  • Double tongue with built-in tolerance

5.2 Add alignment features (blocking) when needed

docs.title: 6. Tolerances
docs.text:
6.1 Account for:

  • Prefinished thickness variation
  • Veneer stability

6.2 Prioritize:

  • Clean exterior alignment
  • Acceptable interior variation where hidden

docs.title: Expectation
docs.text:
• Tongue-and-groove is the default standard
  • Exceptions must be intentional
  • Drawings must clearly show non-standard conditions

docs.title: PURPOSE
docs.text: Ensure assemblies fit correctly before leaving the shop to reduce install issues and rework.

docs.title: RESPONSIBILITY
docs.text:
• Shop (Assembly): Perform fit-up
  • Engineering: Provide clear drawings
  • Project Management: Verify requirement is met when needed

docs.title: Procedure
docs.text:
1. Identify assemblies that require fit-up:

  2. Multi-cabinet runs

  3. Large/tall elevations

  4. Complex joinery or alignment conditions

  5. Dry-fit components in the shop:

  6. Assemble parts to confirm fit and alignment

  7. Verify joints, reveals, and overall dimensions

  8. Adjust as needed:

  9. Correct any fit, alignment, or tolerance issues

  10. Document when required:

  11. Take photos of assembled elevation if requested

  12. Disassemble and prep for shipment

docs.title: Expectation
docs.text:
• Fit-up is completed on all critical assemblies
  • Issues are resolved in-shop, not in the field
  • Assemblies leave the shop ready for efficient installation

docs.title: PURPOSE
docs.text: To define the standardized process for manufacturing and machining 20-minute rated wood fire door frames in compliance with FyreWerks Category C listings.

docs.title: RESPONSIBILITY
docs.text:
• Engineering: Define frame design
  • Production: Build frames
  • Machining: Perform prep
  • QC: Verify compliance

docs.title: 1. Materials
docs.text:
1. Use approved wood species
  2. No finger joints allowed

docs.title: 2. Construction
docs.text:
1. Jamb ≥ 3/4"
  2. Stop height = 1/2"
  3. Rabbet per spec

docs.title: 3. Assembly
docs.text:
1. Frame square
  2. Joints tight

docs.title: 4. Installation Interface
docs.text:
1. Wall thickness ≥ 3-1/2"
  2. Shim ≤ 1/2"

docs.title: 5. Gasketing
docs.text: 1. Install approved gasketing

docs.title: 6. Machining
docs.text: 1. Hinges and strike prep only

docs.title: 7. Labeling
docs.text: 1. Apply WHI label

docs.title: 8. QC
docs.text: 1. Verify materials and dimensions

docs.title: EXPECTATION
docs.text:
• Gasketing is critical
  • No deviations allowed

docs.title: PURPOSE
docs.text: Define the standardized process for manufacturing 20-minute rated slab fire doors in compliance with listing requirements.

docs.title: RESPONSIBILITY
docs.text:
• Engineering: Ensure design meets listing
  • Production: Build per specification
  • Machining: Perform compliant prep
  • QC: Verify compliance and labeling

docs.title: 1. Pre-Production
docs.text:
1. Verify door size and thickness meet listing
  2. Confirm core type and materials per work order

docs.title: 2. Core & Materials
docs.text:
1. Use approved core material
  2. Verify no prohibited joints
  3. Use approved adhesive

docs.title: 3. Layup & Pressing
docs.text:
1. Complete layup per specification
  2. Press per adhesive requirements
  3. Ensure no voids or delamination

docs.title: 4. Faces & Edges
docs.text:
1. Apply face materials per spec
  2. Apply edge banding if required
  3. Ensure no exposed core

docs.title: 5. Machining
docs.text:
1. Perform hinge and lock prep within NFPA 80 limits
  2. No unapproved machining

docs.title: 6. Labeling
docs.text: 1. Apply fire label after QC approval

docs.title: Expectation
docs.text:
• No deviations from listing
  • Door must be square, flat, and defect-free
  • Non-compliant doors must not be labeled

docs.title: PURPOSE
docs.text: To define the standardized process for manufacturing 20-minute rated stile and rail fire doors in compliance with FyreWerks FC-20 Intertek listings, ensuring all doors meet fire-rating, machining, and certification requirements.

docs.title: RESPONSIBILITY
docs.text:
• Engineering
  • Ensure door design complies with FC-20 listing (Category A or B).

  • Define stile & rail construction method and panel layout.

  • Purchasing

  • Procure approved core, intumescent, and face materials.

  • Door Machining Department

  • Perform machining per Intertek machining listing.

  • Ensure compliance with NFPA 80 limitations.

  • Assembly Department

  • Construct doors per approved stile & rail configuration.

  • Quality Control (QC)

  • Verify compliance with listing, materials, and labeling.

docs.title: 1. Listing & Compliance Requirements
docs.text:
1. All doors must comply with:
  2. FyreWerks FC-20 Intertek Listing (Category A or B)
  3. Fire rating:
  4. 20-minute positive pressure with hose stream
  5. Certification:
  6. Must bear “Listed Fire Door” label with WHI mark
  7. Standards:
  8. UL 10B / UL 10C / NFPA 252

docs.title: 2. Door Types (Critical Distinction)
docs.text:
Doors may be constructed as:

  1. Plant-On Stile & Rail Door

  2. Full face over FC-20 core

  3. True Stile & Rail Door

  4. Core exists only in panels
  5. Panels must extend minimum 1/4" into stile

⚠️ This distinction is critical—incorrect panel/core placement voids the rating.

docs.title: 3. Door Size & Thickness Limits
docs.text:
1. Maximum sizes:
  2. Single: 48" x 96"
  3. Pairs: 84" x 96"
  4. Minimum thickness:
  5. 1-3/4" ± 1/16"
  6. No voids allowed in construction

docs.title: 4. Core Requirements
docs.text:
1. Approved cores:
  2. FC-20, FX-20, or FW-20
  3. Core thickness:
  4. Approx. 1/4"
  5. Splicing:
  6. Max 1 splice
  7. Min 4" width lap joint
  8. Core must be labeled as a listed component.

docs.title: 5. Stile & Rail Construction
docs.text:
1. Stiles:
  2. Hardwood, MDF, or engineered lumber
  3. Rails:
  4. Same allowable materials
  5. Fastening:
  6. Dowels/screws allowed only with adhesive

docs.title: 6. Panel & Face Construction
docs.text:
1. Acceptable face materials:
  2. MDF, hardwood, veneer, HPL
  3. Panel layouts:
  4. Must follow approved diagrams

docs.title: Category A
docs.text: • Built into door edges

docs.title: Category B
docs.text: • Requires field-applied edge sealing

docs.title: 8. Adhesives & Pressing
docs.text:
1. Fire-rated adhesives only
  2. Follow manufacturer specs

docs.title: 9. Machining Requirements
docs.text:
1. Must comply with NFPA 80
  2. Max bore: 2-1/8"
  3. Clearances: 1/8"

docs.title: 10. Assembly Process
docs.text:
1. Assemble stiles and rails
  2. Insert panels correctly
  3. Ensure no voids

docs.title: 11. Labeling
docs.text: 1. Apply WHI label

docs.title: 12. QC
docs.text: 1. Verify all requirements

docs.title: EXPECTATION
docs.text:
• Panel/core relationship is critical
  • Machining limits must be followed
  • No deviations allowed

docs.title: PURPOSE
docs.text: To define the standardized process for manufacturing and machining 60-minute rated wood/composite fire door frames in compliance with FyreWerks Intertek Category C listings.

docs.title: RESPONSIBILITY
docs.text:
• Engineering: Define design
  • Production: Build frame
  • Machining: Perform prep
  • QC: Verify compliance

docs.title: 1. Core Selection
docs.text:
1. Use FF or FFX core
  2. Match work order

docs.title: 2. Construction
docs.text:
1. Frame thickness ≥ 3/4"
  2. Frame depth ≥ 4-5/8"
  3. Follow joint rules

docs.title: 3. Rabbet & Stop
docs.text:
1. Rabbet ≥ 1-7/8"
  2. Stop ≥ 1/2"
  3. Stops at 12" OC

docs.title: 4. Face Materials
docs.text:
1. Veneer ≥ 18 pcf
  2. Thickness limits apply

docs.title: 5. Edge Banding
docs.text: 1. Max 3/16"

docs.title: 6. Jamb Extensions
docs.text: 1. Allowed per spec

docs.title: 7. Adhesives
docs.text: 1. Fire-rated only

docs.title: 8. Fire Features
docs.text:
1. Edge sealing required for FFX
  2. Intumescent must be continuous

docs.title: 9. Machining
docs.text: 1. Hinges and strike prep only

docs.title: 10. Labeling
docs.text: 1. Apply certification label

docs.title: 11. QC
docs.text: 1. Verify all requirements

docs.title: EXPECTATION
docs.text:
• Frame is a fire system
  • Edge sealing is critical
  • No deviations allowed

docs.title: 60-Minute Slab Fire Door Manufacturing (Lexington Fire Guard – Spec 17352)
PURPOSE
docs.text: Define the standardized process for manufacturing 60-minute rated slab fire doors in compliance with Intertek Authorization to Mark (Spec 17352).

docs.title: RESPONSIBILITY
docs.text:
• Production: Assemble doors per listed construction.
  • Quality Control (QC): Verify size, rating, and compliance with listed limitations.
  • Engineering: Ensure designs fall within listed constraints and hardware allowances.

docs.title: 1. Pre-Production Verification
docs.text:
1. Confirm door type:
  2. Single swing only
  3. Confirm maximum size:
  4. 4'-0" x 8'-0"
  5. Confirm fire rating:
  6. 60-minute rated
  7. Temperature rise: 250°F @ 60 minutes
  8. Confirm frame compatibility:
  9. Any listed wood, aluminum, or steel frame

docs.title: 2. Core Selection & Verification
docs.text:
1. Use composite Agri-core construction.
  2. Ensure the core type matches:
  3. “Lexington Fire Guard 60 Fire Door; Agri-Core”
  4. Do not substitute alternate cores unless approved under the listing.

Note: Density requirements are not specified and are not part of this SOP.

docs.title: 3. Door Construction
docs.text:
1. Construct door using listed composite core system.
  2. Ensure assembly complies with:
  3. Positive pressure and neutral pressure fire test requirements
  4. Category classification:
  5. Category A (no additional edge sealing required)

docs.title: 4. Hardware Preparation
docs.text:
1. Only use permitted hardware types:
  2. Cylindrical or mortise locks
  3. Card locks
  4. Surface-mounted hardware
  5. Rim exit devices
  6. Concealed closer / door stop
  7. Mortise automatic door bottoms
  8. Electrical strikes
  9. Edge guards
  10. Louvers, viewers
  11. Pivot and continuous hinges
  12. Raceways and dust boxes

  13. Applied moldings

  14. Concealed/invisible hinges:

  15. Allowed up to 45-minute applications only

docs.title: 5. Intumescent / Gasketing Requirements
docs.text:
1. Positive pressure applications:
  2. Intumescent and/or gasketing required per manufacturer instructions.
  3. Neutral pressure applications:
  4. Intumescent not required

docs.title: 6. Assembly & Manufacturing
docs.text:
1. Assemble door per approved composite construction.
  2. Ensure all components align with listing description.
  3. No deviations permitted.

docs.title: 7. Machining & Finishing
docs.text:
1. Perform machining consistent with approved hardware prep.
  2. Ensure no modifications fall outside listed allowances.

docs.title: 8. Traum Trim-Out Instructions
docs.text:
1. For positive pressure doors:
  2. Installation instructions must be provided with the door.
  3. Install:
  4. Intumescent materials
  5. Smoke/draft control gasketing per manufacturer instructions.
  6. Do not omit required sealing components.

docs.title: 9. Labeling
docs.text:
1. Apply Intertek certification mark only when compliant.
  2. Ensure correct rating and designation.

docs.title: 10. Quality Control Checks
docs.text:
1. Verify:
  2. Door size within limits.
  3. Correct core used.
  4. Approved hardware only.
  5. Confirm compliance before labeling.

docs.title: EXPECTATION
docs.text:
• Strict compliance with Spec 17352.
  • No inferred requirements permitted.
  • Non-conforming doors must not be labeled or shipped.

docs.title: PURPOSE
docs.text: To define the standardized process for manufacturing 60-minute rated stile and rail fire doors in compliance with FyreWerks Intertek listings, ensuring all doors meet fire-rating requirements, machining limitations, and certification standards.

docs.title: RESPONSIBILITY
docs.text:
• Engineering
  • Ensure door design complies with FyreWerks FC-60 listing requirements.

  • Define approved construction method (panel layout, edge sealing, intumescent application).

  • Purchasing

  • Procure all materials per approved vendors and work order.

  • Door Machining Department

  • Verify all materials meet listing requirements prior to machining.
  • Perform all machining operations within allowed limits (NFPA 80 + Intertek listing).

  • Ensure no deviations from certified construction.

  • Assembly Department

  • Construct doors per approved stile and rail configuration and bonding requirements.

  • Quality Control (QC)

  • Verify final product meets listing, labeling, and compliance requirements.

docs.title: 1. Listing & Compliance Requirements
docs.text:
1. All 60-minute stile and rail doors must comply with:
  2. FyreWerks FC-60 Intertek Listing (Category A or B depending on application)
  3. Doors must:
  4. Be labeled “Listed Fire Door” with rating and certification mark
  5. Meet UL 10C / NFPA 252 fire test standards
  6. No deviations from listed construction are permitted without formal approval.

docs.title: 2. Door Size & Construction Limits
docs.text:
1. Maximum sizes:
  2. Single doors: 48" x 96"
  3. Pairs: 84" x 96"
  4. Minimum thickness:
  5. 1-3/4" ± 1/16"
  6. No voids are allowed in construction.

docs.title: 3. Core Requirements
docs.text:
1. Core must be:
  2. FyreWerks FC-60 or FX-60 5-ply proprietary core
  3. Core rules:
  4. Minimum thickness: 1/2"
  5. Maximum one splice allowed (lap joint, min. 4" width)
  6. Core must be clearly marked with:
  7. FC-60 / FX-60 and WHI certification identifiers

docs.title: 4. Stile & Rail Construction
docs.text:
1. Stiles:
  2. Internal hardwood stiles permitted
  3. Typical size: 1/2" x 1-1/4" hardwood
  4. Rails:
  5. Not used in MDF-based listed construction (panel construction governs design)
  6. Panel configurations:
  7. Must follow approved FyreWerks layouts (1-panel through multi-panel designs)
  8. Refer to manufacturing diagrams for layouts

docs.title: 5. Face Materials
docs.text:
1. Acceptable face materials:
  2. 5/8" MDF
  3. Hardwood (min. 18 pcf density)
  4. Veneer (1/16")
  5. HPL laminate (0.050")
  6. MDF may be routed for panel profiles per approved designs.

docs.title: 6. Edge Construction & Intumescent
docs.text:
1. Category A (preferred machining method):

  2. Intumescent may be:

    • Concealed in stile
    • Routed into door edges
    • Applied to frame depending on listing

  3. Category B (if used):

  4. Requires field-applied edge sealing system

  5. Approved intumescent:

  6. FyreEdge or Palusol products
  7. Hardwood edges:
  8. Maximum 1/4" thickness to conceal intumescent

docs.title: 7. Adhesives & Pressing
docs.text:
1. Only adhesives approved for 60-minute fire door construction may be used.
  2. Must follow manufacturer requirements for:
  3. Spread rate
  4. Press time
  5. Temperature and pressure
  6. Improper adhesive application voids fire rating.

docs.title: 8. Machining Requirements (Critical Control)
docs.text:
1. All machining must comply with:
  2. NFPA 80

  3. Intertek machining listing

  4. Key limitations:

  5. Max bore: 2-1/8"
  6. Min latch throw: 1/2"

  7. Hardware must be fire-rated and approved

  8. Clearances:

  9. Max 1/8" gap at frame and between leaves

  10. Machining rules:

  11. No unapproved cutouts or modifications
  12. Lite openings max 100 sq. in. (if used)
  13. Minimum spacing from edges must be maintained

docs.title: 9. Assembly Process
docs.text:
1. Layup must follow:
  2. Core centered and properly aligned
  3. Stiles applied per design
  4. Face materials bonded with approved adhesive
  5. Ensure:
  6. No voids
  7. Full adhesive coverage
  8. Press door per adhesive specifications.

docs.title: 10. Hardware & Accessories
docs.text:
1. Allowed hardware includes:
  2. Hinges per NFPA 80
  3. Fire exit devices
  4. Flush bolts, closers, locksets
  5. Restrictions:
  6. Hardware must be listed for fire doors
  7. Install per manufacturer specs

docs.title: 11. Labeling & Certification
docs.text:
1. Each door must:
  2. Receive Intertek certification label
  3. Be traceable to work order and materials
  4. Labels must remain visible after installation.

docs.title: 12. Inspection & Quality Control
docs.text:
1. QC must verify:
  2. Correct core and materials
  3. Proper construction method
  4. Machining compliance
  5. Labeling present
  6. Any deviation:
  7. Door must be rejected or reworked
  8. Cannot be labeled or shipped

docs.title: EXPECTATION
docs.text:
• All 60-minute stile and rail doors must strictly follow FyreWerks listing requirements—no field improvisation is allowed.
  • The Door Machining Department is a critical control point and must ensure all machining stays within certified limits.
  • Materials, construction, and hardware must match the listing exactly to maintain fire rating.
  • Any deviation invalidates certification and is not acceptable for production or shipment.
